metadata.title: Research PPV Collapse with Claude: Why a 99%-Specific Test Is Wrong Two-Thirds of the Time
metadata.color_semantics: teardown: INK #2A1A0E body text; CRIMSON #C8102E key finding/vulnerability; SLATE #545454 structure/neutral
metadata.note: CLI video REQUIRED spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 OUTPUT(slate) · B07 SUMMARY · B08 NEXT STEPS · B09 outro. Source: cancer-research/chapters/01-cancer-screening-finding-it-early-enough-to-cure.md
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)

then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. The test is ninety-nine percent specific. You probably don't have cancer.

A multi-cancer blood test reports ninety-nine percent specificity. The press release calls it a breakthrough. But a positive result in a low-prevalence population means you probably don't have cancer — and the math that explains this gap is never in the press release. The question: what number actually tells you what a positive test means?
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — problem card: 99% specificity headline vs PPV reality; question on screen before terminal opens

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to build the full two-by-two table at three prevalence levels and compute PPV at each.

props.command:
claude "A screening test has sensitivity 90% and specificity
99%. Build a full 2x2 table for 100,000 people at
three prevalence levels: 0.5%, 2%, 10%. Compute PPV
at each level. Then apply this framework to the
Galleri multi-cancer early detection test — what
prevalence does it screen in, what does the literature
say about its PPV in practice, and does the
NHS-Galleri trial give us mortality data or only
detection rate data? Cite sources."

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) — Python script reading Claude response
beats.narration_text: Read it. The script computes PPV from first principles — true positives over true plus false positives. At point-five percent prevalence, out of one hundred thousand people, nine-hundred-ninety-five are true negatives called positive. Only four-hundred-fifty are actual cases. PPV is thirty-one percent.

props.code:
# ppv_calc.py -- Claude Code output
SENS = 0.90; SPEC = 0.99; N = 100_000

for prev in [0.005, 0.02, 0.10]:
    tp = prev * N * SENS          # true positives
    fp = (1-prev) * N * (1-SPEC)  # false positives
    ppv = tp / (tp + fp)
    fn = prev * N * (1-SENS)
    tn = (1-prev) * N * SPEC
    print(f"Prev {prev:.1%}: TP={tp:.0f} FP={fp:.0f}"
          f" FN={fn:.0f} TN={tn:.0f}")
    print(f"  PPV = {ppv:.1%}")
# 0.5%: PPV=31%  2%: PPV=65%  10%: PPV=91%

beats.narration_text: Run it. At point-five percent prevalence, PPV is thirty-one — the test is right less than a third of the time. At two percent, it climbs to sixty-five. At ten percent, ninety-one. Same test, same numbers. Prevalence is the variable nobody quotes.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: B04_PPVGrid — three-bar chart showing PPV at three prevalence levels, bars rising with labels

beats.narration_text: Now add the false-positive burden. Ask Claude to apply Wilson and Jungner criterion five — does the harm of false positives at each prevalence level outweigh the benefit?

props.command:
claude "Apply Wilson and Jungner criterion 5 (benefits
outweigh harms) — what false-positive burden does
each prevalence scenario imply, and what follow-up
workup cascade cost would that represent for a
health system screening one million people?"

beats.narration_text: The harm side. At point-five percent prevalence in one million people, over nine thousand receive false positives — each triggering imaging, biopsy, anxiety, and cost. The test that looks useful in high-risk groups looks expensive and dangerous in low-risk groups. Wilson-Jungner five is not an asterisk; it is the decision.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — false-positive burden table at three prevalence levels; workup cascade cost annotation

beats.narration_text: The test's specificity is not the story. Prevalence is the story. PPV is a joint property of the test and the population you test it in. The same ninety-nine percent specific test that is clinically useful at ten percent prevalence generates mostly false positives at point-five percent prevalence. That number belongs in every press release.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — summary card: PPV = f(test, population); specificity alone is not the story

beats.narration_text: When you read about a new screening test, find three numbers: sensitivity, specificity, and the prevalence in the intended population. Run the two-by-two table. If the PPV is below fifty percent, ask whether the false-positive workup burden has been costed. Those three questions will tell you more than the headline.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — next steps: find sensitivity, specificity, prevalence; run the 2x2; ask about FP burden

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
